title: Pretrained Language Model in Continual Learning: A Comparative Study

structured.summary: The paper conducts an extensive comparative analysis of various continual learning methods applied to pre-trained language models across different benchmarks. It aims to understand the susceptibility of these models to catastrophic forgetting and provide insights into the effectiveness of each method under different CL settings. While offering useful evaluations and raising future research questions, the study doesn't present new methodologies, focusing instead on empirical comparisons.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation of PLMs with CL methods
strengths.evidence: The paper evaluates 5 PLMs and 4 CL method families across two incremental settings on three benchmarks (Section 1).
The study includes analyses of PLMs' performance characteristics in a layer-wise manner, exploring how different CL methods affect each layer (Abstract).

strengths.point: Contribution to understanding catastrophic forgetting in PLMs
strengths.evidence: The experiments highlight performance differences and susceptibilities to forgetting in PLMs (Section 3).

weaknesses.evidence: The paper relies on existing PLMs and CL methods for evaluation without introducing new methodologies (Section 3.1).
The evaluation is conducted on only three benchmark datasets, potentially limiting the general applicability of findings (Section 1).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient Exploration of All CL Settings
weaknesses.evidence: The study focuses on task-incremental and class-incremental settings, largely ignoring domain-incremental learning (Section 2.2).
The paper mentions layer-wise analysis but lacks depth and specific insights (Abstract).
There is no mention of statistical tests to validate performance differences observed among models and methods (Section 3).
There are no details on hyperparameter tuning across different PLM and CL combinations (Section 3.1).
The paper lacks a detailed description of the experimental setup, including computational resources used (Section 1).
The paper does not discuss the computational cost or scalability for large-scale applications (not addressed in the study).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient Justification for Choice of PLMs
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not justify why these particular five PLMs were chosen over other models (Section 2.1).
The paper mentions open research questions but does not clearly specify them (Abstract).
The paper lacks extensive metrics or comparative tables detailing each model and method's performance on benchmarks.
Descriptions of 'interesting, and sometimes surprising, performance differences' are vague without specific examples.

questions.question: How do the different layers within PLMs contribute to their robustness in continual learning?
questions.motivation: Understanding this could inform more targeted strategies to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.

questions.question: Why were newer or additional PLMs not included in the study?
questions.motivation: Justifying model selection is important to understand the comprehensiveness and representativeness of the study.

questions.question: What are the computational implications of integrating these methods in real-world applications?
questions.motivation: Knowing the scalability and computational requirements is essential for practical deployment of these models.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Methodology. The paper relies on existing PLMs and CL methods for evaluation without introducing new methodologies (Section 3.1)
critique_points.point_002: Limited Benchmark Diversity. The evaluation is conducted on only three benchmark datasets, potentially limiting the general applicability of findings (Section 1)
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient Exploration of All CL Settings. The study focuses on task-incremental and class-incremental settings, largely ignoring domain-incremental learning (Section 2.2)
critique_points.point_004: Superficial Analysis of Layer-wise Behavior. The paper mentions layer-wise analysis but lacks depth and specific insights (Abstract)
critique_points.point_005: Inadequate Statistical Rigour. There is no mention of statistical tests to validate performance differences observed among models and methods (Section 3)
critique_points.point_006: Lack of Comprehensive Hyperparameter Tuning. There are no details on hyperparameter tuning across different PLM and CL combinations (Section 3.1)
critique_points.point_007: Potential Reproducibility Challenge. The paper lacks a detailed description of the experimental setup, including computational resources used (Section 1)
critique_points.point_008: Overlooking Real-world Scalability Issues. The paper does not discuss the computational cost or scalability for large-scale applications (not addressed in the study)
critique_points.point_009: Insufficient Justification for Choice of PLMs. The paper does not justify why these particular five PLMs were chosen over other models (Section 2.1)
critique_points.point_010: Vague Open Research Directions. The paper mentions open research questions but does not clearly specify them (Abstract)
critique_points.point_011: Lack of Comparative Results. The paper lacks extensive metrics or comparative tables detailing each model and method's performance on benchmarks
critique_points.point_012: Ambiguity in Performance Reporting. Descriptions of 'interesting, and sometimes surprising, performance differences' are vague without specific examples
